summary:
The circumnavigation of Africa by Phoenician sailors (c. 600 BC) is reported by Herodotus as having been commissioned by the Egyptian pharaoh Necho II. According to Herodotus, Phoenician sailors departed from the Red Sea, sailed south and around Africa, and returned through the Pillars of Hercules (Strait of Gibraltar) to Egypt after approximately three years. The account includes a detail Herodotus himself found unbelievable — that the sun was on the sailors' right hand (north) as they sailed westward around the southern tip of Africa — which modern analysts consider evidence that the event actually occurred, since this is precisely what one would observe south of the equator.

No Phoenician records of this voyage survive independently, and debate continues about its historicity. If genuine, it would represent the first known circumnavigation of Africa by approximately 2,100 years, preceding Vasco da Gama's 1497–1499 voyage. The voyage would have required exceptional navigational skill and endurance, including stopping to grow crops on shore to resupply. The Phoenicians' maritime expertise — they were the dominant Mediterranean seafarers of their era, reaching Britain and West Africa on other recorded voyages — makes the account at least plausible.
